title: Open-Source vs. Closed APIs
concept: Open-source models offer privacy and customization (LoRA fine-tuning); closed APIs offer state-of-the-art accuracy with zero hardware setup.

exercises.prompt: What does it mean to connect to an AI model using an API?

options.text: Downloading a massive model file to your phone.
Sending a text request over the internet to a server that sends back a response.
Writing a machine learning algorithm from scratch.

exercises.explain: An API (Application Programming Interface) allows your app to talk to a model hosted on someone else's server, meaning you don't need to run it locally.

cards.title: The AI Crossroads
cards.body: Should you build your AI project using an open-source model that you control, or plug into a powerful closed API operated by a tech giant? Making the right choice determines how much control you have, how much you pay, and where your data lives!

cards.title: Open-Source vs. Closed APIs
cards.body: Open-Source Models (like Llama or Mistral) let you download the code and run it on your own computer. You can customize them using techniques like LoRA fine-tuning. Closed APIs (like OpenAI or Anthropic) are cloud-based black boxes. You send them text over the internet, and they send back answers, keeping their inner workings hidden.

cards.title: The Garage vs. The Taxi
cards.body: Think of an open-source model like a project car in your garage: you can take it apart, customize the engine, and keep it private, but you have to build and maintain it yourself. A closed API is like ordering a premium taxi ride: it is ready instantly, incredibly high-performing, and you do not need to own a garage—but you must pay for each ride and trust the driver.

cards.title: Choosing the Right Tool
cards.body: If a bank wants to build an AI assistant, they might choose an open-source model run on their own secure local servers so financial data never leaves the building. On the other hand, a solo developer launching a quick prototype tonight might use a closed API to get the absolute smartest model running in 10 minutes with just a few lines of code.

cards.title: The 'Free' Software Trap
cards.body: Many think open-source models are always free. While the code is free to download, running large models requires very expensive computer hardware and energy. Sometimes, paying a closed API per question actually ends up being much cheaper for small projects!

cards.title: Visualizing the Difference
cards.body: Here is how the two approaches compare on key features:

right.text: Top-tier performance, zero setup, pay-per-use, hosted in the cloud.

left.text: Complete privacy, fully customizable, run on your own hardware.

exercises.prompt: What is a major advantage of using closed APIs?

options.text: They require zero hardware setup and offer state-of-the-art accuracy.
They let you download the entire model code and run it offline.
They guarantee complete offline data privacy out of the box.
They are always free to use no matter how many requests you make.

exercises.explain: Closed APIs require zero hardware setup because the model runs on the service provider's cloud servers. You simply send requests over the internet.

exercises.prompt: True or False: If you need 100% data privacy where no information leaves your local servers, open-source models are generally preferred over closed APIs.
exercises.explain: Running open-source models gives you full control over your data, meaning you do not have to send sensitive information to any external companies.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the statement about customizing open-source models.
exercises.sentence: Open-source models can be customized for specific tasks using techniques like [blank] fine-tuning.
exercises.explain: LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) is a popular, lightweight method to fine-tune and customize open-source models on your own data.

exercises.prompt: Identify which of the following is a closed API service rather than an open-source model.
exercises.explain: Option A is a closed API (provided as a service by OpenAI). Options B and C are open-source models that you can download and run on your own hardware.

exercises.prompt: You are building an app for a hospital that handles highly sensitive patient records and needs custom medical terminology. Why might you choose an open-source model over a closed API, or vice versa?

options.text: Using an open-source model means you never have to worry about running out of computer memory or setting up servers.
Closed APIs require you to download huge model files and buy expensive graphics cards before you can use them.
Open-source models offer complete privacy and customization, while closed APIs offer instant access to top performance with zero setup.
Open-source models are always much smarter and more accurate than closed APIs for every single task.

exercises.explain: Open-source models give you complete ownership of your data and model architecture (great for strict privacy or fine-tuning), but require you to manage your own servers. Closed APIs handle the massive hardware for you and give you instant access to the most powerful models, but you must send your data to external servers and pay per usage.
